svg.text: Fine-Grained Quantization
OUTLIER ISOLATION • GROUP-BASED SCALING • INDEPENDENT SCALE FACTORS
DeepSeek Quantization Technique — Part 3 of 6
PRECISION CONTROL
The Problem: Outliers Collapse Quantization Quality
Standard FP8 quantization uses a single scale factor for an entire tensor:
scale = max_fp8_val / max(|tensor|)
If even one outlier value exists in the tensor, it forces an extremely small scale factor, compressing all
other normal values into a tiny portion of the FP8 range — destroying their representational precision.
EXAMPLE TENSOR:
→ 0.3 × 3.5 ≈ 1 (loses all precision!)
DeepSeek Solution: Independent Scale Factors per Group
Divide tensors into small groups. Each group computes its own scale factor independently.
An outlier in one group only affects that group's scale — all other groups remain accurately quantized.
Group Size Specifications
Activation Groups: 1 × 128
Each row of 128 consecutive elements shares one scale factor

g.text: Outlier in Row 0 → only s₀ is affected. Rows 1,2,3... have optimal, high-quality scales s₁,s₂,s₃
The rest of the matrix maintains full quantization quality!
Each row independently: no cross-contamination from outliers in other rows

svg.text: Weight Groups: 128 × 128
Each 128×128 tile of the weight matrix gets its own scale

g.text: Tile C's small scale has zero effect on A, B, D, E — each tile independent

svg.text: Standard vs Fine-Grained Quantization — Visual Comparison
Standard Quantization (One Scale)
Single scale
FP8 range coverage:
all other values (0.1 to 0.8) crammed into 1-3 FP8 levels
Problem: Normal values 0.1–0.8 all map to same FP8 level ≈ 1
Cannot distinguish 0.2 from 0.8 — these become the same quantized value!
Lost information → degraded model quality → slow convergence
Memory cost: 1 scale per tensor (negligible)
Fine-Grained Quantization (Per-Group Scale)
Group 1 and Group 2: full FP8 range used for 0.1–0.8 values!
Can distinguish 0.2 from 0.8 → precision restored for 127 of 128 groups
Only Group 0 (the outlier group) pays a precision cost
Overhead: 1 FP32 scale per 128 elements = 3.1% memory overhead (negligible)
Step-by-Step Quantization Mechanism

g.text: s = 448.0 / max(abs(group)) — using E4M3 max value 448
fp8_val = round(fp32_val × s) — maps to integer in [-448, 448]
Scale s stored in FP32 alongside quantized data for later recovery
fp32_approx = fp8_val / s — multiply output by 1/s to recover scale
→ values 0.1-0.8 map to FP8 ≈ 0-2 levels only
→ 0.1 maps to FP8 56, 0.8 maps to FP8 448
Result: 160× more effective precision for non-outlier groups
scale 3.5 → scale 560 for the same value range (560/3.5 = 160×)
Memory overhead: 1 FP32 scale per 128 values = 3.1% extra
Tradeoff: tiny memory cost → massive precision gain

svg.text: Key Takeaways — Fine-Grained Quantization

g.text: Outliers in one group cannot corrupt other groups.
Each 128-element slice independently optimizes its scale.
Activation rows and weight tiles chosen to balance
Fine-grained scaling covers E4M3's limited range (448).
This is WHY DeepSeek can choose E4M3 over E5M2.
Scale storage = 3.1% extra memory per tensor.
Negligible cost for massive quality improvement.
Simple per-group max computation added before
each GEMM — no complex calibration needed.
Without fine-grained quantization, FP8 training would
suffer significant quality loss vs BF16.

svg.text: Fine-grained quantization + Online scaling + E4M3 = FP8 quality that matches BF16 baseline
